title: Dictionaries: A Reading Is More Than One Number
learningObjectives: Represent a single structured record with multiple named fields using a dictionary instead of several separate variables
Access, add, and update dictionary values by key, including safely with .get()
Iterate over a list of dictionaries (records) with a for loop
Recognize when a dictionary is the right structure versus when a plain list is enough
whyItMatters: A single demand_kw number isn't a full sensor record — a real reading also has a timestamp, a location, maybe a data-quality flag. Every weather and price API response Mission 2 consumes arrives as exactly this kind of structured, labeled record, and dictionaries are how Python represents it before it ever reaches pandas.
capstoneContribution: The structured reading-record format ({'timestamp': ..., 'demand_kw': ..., 'source': ...}) the Digital Twin uses for every ingested data point, and the direct shape Mission 2's API responses will arrive in.

problem.summary: A bare number doesn't say when or where it was measured.
problem.content: A single `demand_kw` number tells you nothing about *when* or *where* it was measured. You need to attach a timestamp and a source to every reading without creating three more separate lists you have to keep in sync by position.

motivation.summary: This is exactly the shape every real weather and price API returns.
motivation.content: This is exactly the shape of data every real weather and price API returns: a labeled bundle of fields per record, not a bare number. Learning dictionaries now means Mission 2's API lesson feels familiar instead of like an entirely new data model.

theory.summary: Dictionaries map keys to values; a list of dictionaries represents many structured records.
theory.content: A dictionary maps keys to values: `reading = {"timestamp": "2026-07-01T09:00", "demand_kw": 4821, "source": "substation-12"}`. Access by key (`reading["demand_kw"]`), add or update a key (`reading["flag"] = "ok"`), and use `.get()` to access a key that might not exist without raising an error. A list of dictionaries (`[reading_1, reading_2, ...]`) represents many structured records — the shape nearly every real dataset takes before it becomes a pandas DataFrame.

visualExplanation.summary: A labeled record card replaces the earlier unlabeled row-of-boxes list.
visualExplanation.content: The diagram shows a labeled record card with named fields (timestamp, demand_kw, source) as opposed to the earlier unlabeled row-of-boxes list, then a stack of these cards representing a list of records.

interactiveDemo.summary: Build a record card, add a second one, and loop over the resulting list.
interactiveDemo.content: Build a record by dragging labeled field/value pairs into a card, then add a second card to a growing list and run a loop that prints one field from each card.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: build, update, and loop over structured reading records.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
reading = {"timestamp": "2026-07-01T09:00", "demand_kw": 4821, "source": "substation-12"}
print(reading["demand_kw"])
reading["flag"] = "ok"

readings = [reading, {"timestamp": "2026-07-01T10:00", "demand_kw": 4790, "source": "substation-12"}]
for r in readings:
    print(r["timestamp"], r["demand_kw"])
```

independentCoding.summary: Build a list of battery-reading dictionaries and filter it in a loop.
independentCoding.content: Given three raw (timestamp, soc_percent) pairs, build a list of dictionaries, one per battery reading, then write a loop that prints only the readings where `soc_percent` is below 25.

debugging.summary: Fix a KeyError caused by assuming every record has the same keys.
debugging.content: One record in a list is missing the `"source"` key, and a loop that does `r["source"]` on every record raises a `KeyError` on that one. Fix the code using `.get()` with a sensible default instead of assuming every record is shaped identically.

reflection.summary: Real APIs don't guarantee every record has the same keys.
reflection.content: Why will assuming every API response has exactly the same keys eventually break your Mission 2 code, and what did `.get()` just teach you about defending against that in advance?

teachBack.summary: Explain why a dictionary beats a bare list for one structured reading.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone who only knows lists why `{"demand_kw": 4821}` is a better fit than a bare list `[4821, "substation-12", "2026-07-01T09:00"]` for representing a single structured reading.

miniProject.summary: Rebuild the rolling window as a list of dictionaries instead of bare numbers.
miniProject.content: Rebuild the sensor-intake mini project's rolling window as a list of dictionaries (one per hour, each with timestamp, demand_kw, and classification) instead of a bare list of numbers, and print a formatted report line per record.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This structure is exactly what the Digital Twin stores and later reads with pandas.
portfolioEvidence.content: This list-of-dictionaries structure becomes the exact shape the Digital Twin's ingestion pipeline stores in SQL (Mission 2) and later reads with pandas.

labs.title: Structured Record
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise building and filtering a list of dictionaries representing sensor readings, including at least one record with a missing key to force correct use of .get().

simulations.title: Dictionary Record Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows a labeled record card with key/value pairs, and a stack of cards for a list of records, with live key lookups highlighted.

flashcards.front: What happens if you access reading["source"] on a dictionary that doesn't have a "source" key?
flashcards.back: It raises a KeyError. Using reading.get("source", "unknown") instead returns a default value rather than crashing when the key is missing.

flashcards.front: Why is {'demand_kw': 4821, 'timestamp': '...'} usually a better fit than a bare list for one sensor reading?
flashcards.back: A dictionary labels each field by name, so code that reads it doesn't depend on remembering a fixed position order — a list like [4821, '...'] only works if every reader agrees on which position means what.

rubric: Explanation correctly identifies that dictionary keys provide meaning that positional list indices don't
Correctly uses .get() with a default in the debugging fix rather than wrapping access in a bare try/except as a first resort
Independent coding filter correctly handles the structured records without assuming a fixed field order
